title: Heliomeris hispida
intro: Heliomeris hispida, commonly known as prairie heliotrope, is a perennial herbaceous plant native to North America, characterized by its yellow or orange daisy-like flowers and hairy stems. The plant has been traditionally used to treat various health issues, including fever, cough, and rheumatism, due to its anti-inflammatory, antiseptic, and expectorant properties. The bioactive constituents of Heliomeris hispida include sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The plant is often used to make herbal preparations such as teas, infusions, and tinctures, as well as being incorporated into traditional remedies for its purported health benefits.
taxonomy: Heliomeris hispida is classified as a species of flowering plant in the sunflower family (Asteraceae). It is a member of the genus Heliomeris, which includes several species of plants native to North America. The genus Heliomeris is further classified under the tribe Heliantheae, a group of sunflowers and related plants. The species Heliomeris hispida is also known as the hispid gaillardia, which is a nod to its appearance and growth habits. Its classification reflects its relationships with other plants in the sunflower family.
benefits: Heliomeris hispida, commonly known as the silverling or hairy bells, has been used in traditional medicine for its various health benefits. The plant is said to possess anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties, which can help in soothing skin irritations and wounds. Its leaves and flowers are also used to treat digestive issues, such as diarrhea and indigestion. Additionally, the plant is believed to have antioxidant properties, which can help protect against cell damage and oxidative stress. More research is needed to confirm the full extent of its health benefits.

Flavonoids, in particular, have been shown to exhibit antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities.
